summary: Concept-rich chapter with only 2 existing figures serving 2 of its ~6 core intuitions (the memorization drivers and the attack ladder). The two most heavily-emphasized ideas in the chapter are visually unserved: (a) why average MIA accuracy is the WRONG metric and TPR@low-FPR on a log-log ROC is the right one (flagged as a 'frequent interview trap', a pitfall box, AND a key takeaway), and (b) the DP-SGD mechanism (per-example clip + Gaussian noise) that is the chapter's only formal defense. A third, canary exposure as a rank-based leakage meter, is the chapter's central measuring-stick and is also unserved. I keep the attack-ladder as-is, refine the scaling figure's dark-theme annotation legibility, and propose 3 new static figures for the unserved intuitions.
core_intuitions: Memorization is the OPTIMAL behavior of maximum-likelihood on rare, high-surprise sequences (no generalizable rule exists, so the only way to drive loss down is to store the string in the weights) - and it is decoupled from classical overfitting because it concerns the tail of rare examples, not the average.
Three factors drive how much a string is memorized: duplication (the biggest lever, ~log n, super-linear on extractability), model scale, and context/prompt length.
The attack ladder is MIA -> extraction -> reconstruction, ordered by how much each recovers and how targeted it is.

Membership-inference success must be read as TPR at very low FPR on a log-log ROC, NOT average accuracy - a model that looks ~chance on average can still confidently leak membership for the most-vulnerable tail records, which is exactly the worst case regulators and plaintiffs surface.

Canary exposure = log2|R| - log2(rank) is a continuous, calibrated leakage meter: it measures how far the true secret has risen toward rank 1 among all candidate fill-ins, and it climbs smoothly during training so you catch a leak before verbatim emission.

DP-SGD = per-example gradient clipping (bound any one record's influence to radius C) + calibrated Gaussian noise, yielding an (epsilon,delta) worst-case bound on EVERY membership adversary; DP fine-tuning >> DP pretraining, and the noise/clip tax falls hardest on the rare tail.

existing.name: privacy-memorization-scaling
existing.issue: The central annotation 'gap = extra memorization from scale' is rendered in a dark ink that sits dark-on-dark in the dark theme and is nearly illegible against the near-black background (verified in the dark render). The curves, axes, and 'larger/smaller model' labels are theme-aware and fine; only this inner annotation fails.
existing.improvement: Make the 'gap = extra memorization from scale' annotation theme-aware (use the same currentColor/neutral-ink token the axis labels use, or place it on a subtle theme-aware pill/halo so it reads on both backgrounds). Optionally nudge it into the shaded gap region with a thin leader line so it clearly labels the wedge between the two curves. No other changes - the log-x duplication axis, the two model curves, and the shaded gap correctly convey drivers 1 (duplication, log-x) and 2 (scale = the gap).

new_figures.name: mia-tpr-low-fpr-roc
new_figures.concept: Why average MIA accuracy hides the worst-case: TPR@low-FPR on a log-log ROC is the privacy-correct metric.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single most-emphasized methodological point in the whole chapter - it appears as a 'frequent interview trap' in 2.2, as an explicit pitfall warning box, and as a key takeaway - yet it has no visual. The intuition ('a model can look ~chance on average and still leak confidently in the low-FPR tail') is inherently graphical and clicks instantly on a log-log ROC where the eye sees a curve hug the diagonal in the bulk but leap above it at the far-left. A static frame nails it.
new_figures.brief: A log-log ROC plot making the case that average accuracy hides worst-case leakage. Axes: X = False Positive Rate, Y = True Positive Rate, BOTH on log scales spanning 1e-3 to 1 (tick labels 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1). Draw the chance line TPR=FPR as a dashed neutral 'random guessing' diagonal from bottom-left to top-right (label it 'chance'). Plot TWO attack curves that start at the same point: (A) the DANGEROUS attack - a curve that hugs the chance diagonal through the mid/high-FPR region (so its area-under-curve looks near-chance, i.e. 'looks private on average') but that at the far LEFT (FPR = 0.001) spikes WELL ABOVE the diagonal, reaching a TPR roughly 50-100x the chance value there; annotate this spike with a callout '~100x chance TPR here = a real breach for the most vulnerable records'. (B) a genuinely-private attack that sits ON the chance diagonal everywhere (label 'truly private: no tail'). Add a second callout over the mid-region of curve A: 'average accuracy ~ chance -> looks private'. A short banner or arrow labels the shaded far-left column 'the tail that matters (low FPR)'. Payoff the reader must see: the SAME curve reads as near-chance in the bulk yet leaps up in the low-FPR tail, so the metric you choose changes the verdict. Constraints: only illustrative axis integers (0.001/0.01/0.1/1) and symbolic annotations ('~100x chance') - invent NO benchmark AUC or TPR numbers as claimed results. Theme-aware ink for axes, curves, and callouts; the chance diagonal and the dangerous curve must be visually distinguishable in both light and dark (e.g. dashed neutral vs solid accent). Everything inside the viewBox, clear reading order left-to-right along FPR.

new_figures.name: dp-sgd-clip-then-noise
new_figures.concept: DP-SGD mechanism: per-example gradient clipping bounds any one record's influence, then Gaussian noise makes membership undetectable -> (epsilon,delta)-DP.
new_figures.rationale: DP-SGD is the chapter's only formal, worst-case defense and its mechanism is described purely in equations (per-example clip to radius C, then add N(0, sigma^2 C^2 I)). The geometric intuition - long outlier gradients get projected onto a radius-C ball so no single example can push harder than C, and noise blurs the sum - is exactly what makes 'DP caps every membership adversary' and 'DP hits the tail hardest' click. It is foundational and currently unserved by any figure.
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right 4-stage geometric flow that turns the DP-SGD equations into a picture. STAGE 1 'Per-example gradients': from a single common origin draw ~5 gradient arrows of different directions and lengths; make ONE arrow conspicuously long and label it 'a rare/outlier example -> large, distinctive gradient'; overlay a dashed circle of radius C centered at the origin (label the circle 'clip ball, radius C'). STAGE 2 'Clip to radius C': redraw the same arrows but every arrow longer than C is shortened to land exactly on the circle boundary (projected inward, same direction), while arrows already inside C are unchanged; annotate with the formula g_tilde_i = g_i * min(1, C / ||g_i||) and the caption 'no single example can push harder than C'. STAGE 3 'Sum + Gaussian noise': show the clipped arrows composed tip-to-tail into one resultant sum arrow, then a soft fuzzy noise cloud / scattered dots added at its tip, labeled N(0, sigma^2 C^2 I); caption 'noise makes any one record's presence undetectable'. STAGE 4 'Noisy averaged step': the final update arrow (divided by batch size B) that the optimizer actually takes, drawn slightly perturbed from the true sum direction. Bottom synthesis caption spanning the stages: 'clipping bounds influence + noise blurs it -> (epsilon,delta)-DP: a worst-case bound on every membership attack'. Optionally a small side note near stage 2: 'the tail (long gradients) is clipped most -> DP taxes rare examples hardest'. Use ONLY symbolic labels (C, sigma, B, epsilon, delta) - no numbers. Theme-aware ink; the clip ball, the outlier arrow, the noise cloud, and the final step arrow must all be legible in both themes. Keep all four stages inside the viewBox with clear left-to-right reading order.

new_figures.name: canary-exposure-rank-ladder
new_figures.concept: Canary exposure = log2|R| - log2(rank): the true secret's rank among all candidate fill-ins rises toward 1 as memorization grows, and exposure is a smooth leakage meter.
new_figures.rationale: Section 3's whole audit methodology rests on the exposure metric, and the chapter leans on it in a worked example and a key takeaway ('watch it during training as an early-warning signal'). The rank-among-candidates -> exposure mapping is abstract in prose but obvious as a picture: a ladder of candidate fill-ins with the true canary sliding from the middle (safe) to the top (extractable). This is the chapter's central measuring-stick and is unserved.
new_figures.placement: 3. Auditing Privacy: Canaries and the Secret Sharer
new_figures.brief: Two side-by-side panels sharing a vertical 'rank ladder' metaphor, with an exposure meter beside each. The ladder is a tall vertical stack of faint rows representing ALL |R| candidate fill-ins for a canary template shown at top (e.g. 'the access code is ____', body = random digits, with a small note '|R| candidate fill-ins, e.g. 10^8'); the ladder is ordered top = rank 1 (lowest perplexity / model's most-likely fill-in) down to bottom = rank |R|. In each panel highlight the ONE row that is the TRUE inserted canary as a distinct, clearly-marked token. LEFT panel titled 'Not memorized': the true-canary row sits near the MIDDLE of the ladder (rank ~ |R|/2); its exposure meter (a vertical fill bar calibrated from 1 bit at the bottom to log2|R| bits at the top) reads near the BOTTOM (~1 bit); caption 'rank ~ |R|/2 -> exposure ~ 1 bit -> safe'. RIGHT panel titled 'Memorized': the SAME true-canary row has risen to the TOP (rank 1); its exposure meter reads FULL (= log2|R| bits); caption 'rank 1 -> exposure = log2|R| -> brute-force extractable'. Between or beneath the panels, display the formula prominently: exposure = log2|R| - log2(rank), and draw a bold arrow pointing left->right labeled 'more duplication / more training' to show what drives the true canary up the ladder. Emphasize that exposure is a smooth dial (the meter is continuous), not a binary. Keep |R| symbolic (write '|R|' and optionally 'e.g. 10^8' / 'log2|R| ~ 26.6 bits' as an illustrative aside, not a claimed result). Theme-aware ink; the highlighted true-canary row and the exposure fill bar must stand out in both light and dark. Both panels and the formula inside the viewBox, clear left-to-right reading (safe -> extractable).
